decision: Three separate predicted mocks (kind:topic), 30Q each; use each group note as the test description.

papers.note: Predicted questions built from recurring past-paper and recall patterns — the themes most likely to reappear. Educated forecasts, not official questions; verify against your lecturers’ material.

questions.explain: Breslow thickness (depth in mm from the granular layer to the deepest tumour cell) is the strongest prognostic factor, together with ulceration and mitotic rate. Clark level is now largely historical.
questions.q: The single most important prognostic factor in cutaneous melanoma is:

questions.explain: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Colour variegation, Diameter >6 mm, Evolution. A changing lesion is one of the most important warning signs.
questions.q: In the ABCDE rule for melanoma, the “E” stands for:

questions.explain: AK is an intra-epidermal dysplasia driven by cumulative UV exposure; a proportion progress to invasive SCC (part of the SCC-in-situ spectrum). It is considered premalignant and cannot itself metastasise.
options: A benign lesion with no malignant potential
A premalignant lesion that may progress to squamous cell carcinoma
A subtype of basal cell carcinoma
questions.q: Actinic (solar) keratosis is best described as:

questions.explain: SCC can metastasise — most often first to regional lymph nodes. Risk is higher on lip/ear, in immunosuppressed patients, and with poor differentiation. (BCC, by contrast, almost never metastasises.)
questions.q: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma most commonly metastasises via:

questions.explain: Nodular BCC: pearly/translucent papule, rolled telangiectatic edge, may ulcerate centrally (“rodent ulcer”). It is the commonest skin cancer — locally invasive but rarely metastasising.
questions.q: A slowly growing pearly papule with a rolled border and surface telangiectasia on the face is most typical of:

questions.explain: Nikolsky (lateral pressure shears the epidermis) is positive in pemphigus (intra-epidermal acantholysis) and in SJS/TEN. It is negative in bullous pemphigoid, where blisters are sub-epidermal and tense.
questions.q: A positive Nikolsky sign is characteristically found in:

questions.explain: PV = IgG against desmoglein 3 (± dsg1) in desmosomes → intra-epidermal acantholysis, flaccid blisters and painful oral erosions. DIF shows intercellular “chicken-wire” IgG/C3.
questions.q: The autoantibodies in pemphigus vulgaris are directed against:

questions.explain: BP = linear IgG/C3 along the dermo-epidermal junction (targets BP180/BP230 in hemidesmosomes). Presents in the elderly with itchy, tense blisters; mucosa usually spared.
options: Linear IgG/C3 along the basement membrane
Granular IgA in the dermal papillae
questions.q: Direct immunofluorescence in bullous pemphigoid classically shows:

questions.explain: DH = intensely pruritic grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces; DIF shows granular IgA in dermal papillae. It is the cutaneous expression of gluten-sensitive enteropathy — treat with a gluten-free diet ± dapsone.
questions.q: Dermatitis herpetiformis is most strongly associated with:

questions.explain: Psoriatic nails show pitting, onycholysis, “oil-drop” (salmon) patches and subungual hyperkeratosis. Nail involvement is a marker of increased psoriatic arthritis risk.
questions.q: Which nail change is most characteristic of psoriasis and correlates with psoriatic arthritis?

questions.explain: Removing the silvery scale of a psoriatic plaque exposes dilated dermal papillary capillaries → pinpoint bleeding (Auspitz sign). Psoriasis shows the Koebner phenomenon too.
questions.q: The Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scale is removed) is seen in:

questions.explain: Acute cutaneous lupus = photosensitive malar/butterfly erythema that characteristically spares the nasolabial folds. Heliotrope and Gottron papules belong to dermatomyositis.
options: A malar (butterfly) rash sparing the nasolabial folds
A heliotrope rash of the eyelids
A herald patch followed by a Christmas-tree pattern
questions.q: The characteristic sun-exposure–related eruption of systemic lupus erythematosus is:

questions.explain: Dermatomyositis: violaceous heliotrope eyelid rash and Gottron papules over the MCP/IP joints, plus proximal myopathy. In adults it carries an association with underlying malignancy.
questions.q: Gottron papules and a heliotrope rash are diagnostic clues to:

questions.explain: Primary syphilis = a solitary, painless, indurated chancre at the inoculation site. (Painful ulcers suggest chancroid/HSV; condylomata lata are moist secondary-stage papules.)
questions.q: The painless, indurated genital ulcer of primary syphilis is called a:

questions.explain: The triad is interstitial keratitis, notched Hutchinson incisors and eighth-nerve deafness. Neurosyphilis and aortitis are late acquired complications, not part of the triad.
questions.q: Hutchinson’s triad of congenital syphilis comprises interstitial keratitis, Hutchinson teeth and:

questions.explain: In a first infestation, symptoms of Sarcoptes scabiei take about 4–6 weeks to appear — a delayed hypersensitivity to the mite. Look for burrows in finger web spaces; treat with topical permethrin.
options: The pruritus typically begins within hours of infestation
Itch and rash reflect a delayed hypersensitivity reaction developing over ~4–6 weeks
It is caused by a louse
questions.q: Which is TRUE of scabies?

questions.explain: Crusted scabies = huge mite burden with thick hyperkeratotic crusts, occurring in the immunosuppressed (HIV, elderly, corticosteroids). It is highly contagious and may need oral ivermectin plus topical therapy.
questions.q: Crusted (Norwegian) scabies is most typically seen in:

questions.explain: Prolonged potent topical steroids cause atrophy, striae, telangiectasia, perioral dermatitis, tachyphylaxis and (on the face) a steroid-rosacea/acneiform eruption.
options: Skin atrophy with striae and telangiectasia
questions.q: A recognised cutaneous side-effect of potent topical corticosteroids is:

questions.explain: Oral isotretinoin is highly teratogenic — pregnancy prevention is mandatory. Monitor lipids and liver enzymes; other effects include cheilitis, dryness, myalgia and mood changes.
options: Hepatotoxic and requires weekly liver biopsy
A potent teratogen requiring reliable contraception
questions.q: Isotretinoin for severe acne requires strict precautions mainly because it is:

questions.explain: Acne pathogenesis = follicular hyperkeratinisation, androgen-driven sebum, C. acnes colonisation and inflammation. Anti-desmoglein antibodies belong to pemphigus, not acne.
questions.q: Which factor is NOT part of the pathogenesis of acne vulgaris?

questions.explain: Rosacea has no comedones (unlike acne) and features central-face flushing, erythema, telangiectasia, papules/pustules and (late) rhinophyma. Ocular involvement is common.
options: Absence of comedones with a background of flushing/telangiectasia
questions.q: A feature that distinguishes rosacea from acne vulgaris is:

questions.explain: EN is a septal panniculitis — tender erythematous nodules on the shins that resolve like a bruise without ulceration. Triggers: streptococcal infection, sarcoidosis, IBD, TB, drugs (e.g. sulfonamides, OCP).
options: A septal panniculitis presenting as tender red nodules on the shins
A vesicular eruption of the trunk
questions.q: Erythema nodosum is best characterised as:

questions.explain: Basal cell carcinoma is the most common skin cancer (and the most common human cancer). SCC is second; melanoma is far less common but causes most skin-cancer deaths.
questions.q: The commonest malignant tumour of the skin overall is:

questions.explain: Melanoma risk rises with numerous / atypical naevi, fair skin, light eyes, red-blond hair, family/personal history and intermittent blistering sunburn. Dark eyes and darker phototypes are relatively protective.
options: A high number of atypical (dysplastic) naevi
questions.q: Which is a recognised risk factor for melanoma?

questions.explain: BP antibodies target the hemidesmosome proteins BP180 (collagen XVII) and BP230, producing a sub-epidermal split — hence tense blisters and a negative Nikolsky sign.
questions.q: The autoantibody target in bullous pemphigoid is a component of the:

questions.explain: Urticaria (transient wheals lasting <24 h, histamine-mediated) is treated first with non-sedating H1 antihistamines, up-dosed as needed. Watch for associated angio-oedema.
questions.q: First-line drug treatment for acute urticaria is:

questions.explain: BCC arises from basal keratinocytes, is locally destructive but almost never metastasises. Main risk factor is chronic UV exposure; commonest on the head and neck.
options: It is locally invasive but very rarely metastasises
questions.q: Which statement about basal cell carcinoma is correct?

questions.explain: Intra-epidermal split with acantholysis, flaccid blisters and prominent mucosal involvement = pemphigus vulgaris. Untreated it can be life-threatening; needs systemic immunosuppression.
questions.q: A patient develops widespread flaccid blisters and painful mouth erosions; biopsy shows intra-epidermal acantholysis. The most likely diagnosis is:

questions.explain: Non-treponemal tests (VDRL/RPR) are quantitative and fall with successful treatment, so they are used to monitor activity. Treponemal tests (TPHA, FTA-ABS) usually stay positive for life.
questions.q: Which laboratory test is used to monitor treatment response / activity in syphilis?

questions.explain: Dupilumab targets IL-4Rα, blocking IL-4 and IL-13 signalling (the Th2 axis central to atopic dermatitis). It has transformed treatment of moderate-to-severe eczema and is also used in asthma.
options: The shared IL-4/IL-13 receptor α subunit
questions.q: Dupilumab, increasingly used in severe atopic dermatitis, is a monoclonal antibody that blocks:

papers.subtitle: High-probability topics · 30 MCQs
papers.title: Prediction Paper 1

papers.note: A balanced mock spanning the whole syllabus at exam-style difficulty. Predicted content, not official questions.

questions.explain: From the basement membrane upward: stratum basale → spinosum → granulosum → (lucidum) → corneum. Keratinocytes mature and flatten as they ascend, forming the protective cornified layer.
questions.q: The layers of the epidermis from deep to superficial are:

questions.explain: Melanocytes (neural-crest derived, basal layer) make melanin in melanosomes and transfer it to keratinocytes to shield nuclear DNA from UV. Langerhans cells are antigen-presenting; Merkel cells mediate touch.
questions.q: The pigment-producing cell of the epidermis, located in the basal layer, is the:

questions.explain: ACD is a type IV delayed hypersensitivity, sensitising over ~48–72 h and diagnosed by patch testing. Common allergens: nickel, fragrances, rubber, para-phenylenediamine.
questions.q: Allergic contact dermatitis is an example of which hypersensitivity reaction?

questions.explain: Pityriasis rosea: a single “herald patch” precedes an eruption following skin cleavage lines (“Christmas-tree” on the back). It is self-limiting over 6–8 weeks; systemic retinoids are not indicated.
questions.q: A herald patch followed days later by a “Christmas-tree” distribution of oval scaly plaques on the trunk suggests:

questions.explain: Lichen planus: the “6 Ps” — pruritic, purple, polygonal, planar (flat-topped) papules/plaques — with lacy white Wickham striae; may involve oral mucosa and nails, and shows the Koebner phenomenon.
questions.q: Wickham striae — fine white lines on the surface of violaceous flat-topped papules — are characteristic of:

questions.explain: Overgrowth of commensal Malassezia yeast produces hypo-/hyperpigmented scaly macules on the trunk; KOH shows a “spaghetti-and-meatballs” pattern of hyphae and spores.
questions.q: Pityriasis (tinea) versicolor is caused by:

questions.explain: Erythrasma fluoresces coral-red (coproporphyrin). Some scalp Microsporum tineas fluoresce green; vitiligo shows accentuated depigmentation; pseudomonas may fluoresce green.
questions.q: On Wood’s light examination, which finding is expected in erythrasma (Corynebacterium minutissimum)?

questions.explain: Molluscum (a poxvirus) causes pearly, umbilicated papules; common in children and in HIV. Anogenital warts (HPV) are verrucous; “target” lesions suggest erythema multiforme.
questions.q: Which is the classic morphology of molluscum contagiosum?

questions.explain: Zoster = reactivation of varicella-zoster virus in a sensory ganglion → painful dermatomal vesicles. Complications include post-herpetic neuralgia and, occasionally, segmental motor weakness.
options: A vesicular eruption confined to a single dermatome
questions.q: Shingles (herpes zoster) is characterised by:

questions.explain: Impetigo (honey-coloured crusts) is usually due to S. aureus, sometimes S. pyogenes. Bullous impetigo is caused by staphylococcal exfoliative toxin.
questions.q: Impetigo is most commonly caused by:

questions.explain: Erysipelas = superficial dermal streptococcal infection with a sharply demarcated, raised border (deeper, less-defined = cellulitis). Systemic upset is common; treat with penicillin-class antibiotics.
questions.q: A well-demarcated, tender, spreading erythema with a raised advancing edge on the lower leg, with fever, suggests:

questions.explain: Alopecia areata = autoimmune, non-scarring round patches with tapering “exclamation-mark” hairs at the margin; nail pitting may coexist. Telogen effluvium is diffuse post-stress shedding; androgenetic alopecia causes patterned recession.
options: Scarring patches with loss of follicular openings
Diffuse shedding 3 months after a stressor
questions.q: Alopecia areata is best recognised by:

questions.explain: A stressor (childbirth, illness, surgery, crash diet) shifts follicles synchronously into telogen; diffuse shedding follows months later and usually recovers spontaneously.
options: Diffuse non-scarring hair shedding ~2–4 months after a physiological stressor
Broken hairs of irregular length from pulling
questions.q: Telogen effluvium classically presents as:

questions.explain: Pyogenic granuloma (lobular capillary haemangioma) is a benign, friable, rapidly growing red nodule that bleeds easily; common in pregnancy and after trauma.
questions.q: Which tumour is a benign vascular lesion that often bleeds and grows rapidly, frequently after minor trauma?

questions.explain: Seborrhoeic keratoses are common benign keratinocyte proliferations — warty, greasy, “stuck-on” pigmented papules. A sudden shower of them (Leser-Trélat sign) can rarely signal internal malignancy.
options: Benign epidermal lesions with a “stuck-on” greasy appearance
questions.q: Seborrhoeic keratoses are:

questions.explain: Acquired naevi evolve through junctional → compound → intradermal phases as nests migrate into the dermis. Most are benign; changing or atypical lesions warrant review.
options: They pass through junctional → compound → intradermal phases over time
They are premalignant and should all be excised
questions.q: Regarding melanocytic naevi, which statement is correct?

questions.explain: SJS/TEN is usually a severe drug reaction (sulfonamides, carbamazepine/lamotrigine, allopurinol, NSAIDs) with mucosal erosions and skin detachment. TEN = >30% body-surface detachment; Nikolsky positive.
questions.q: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis is most often triggered by:

questions.explain: SJS <10% BSA, SJS/TEN overlap 10–30%, TEN >30% epidermal detachment. Higher detachment = higher mortality; management is supportive, often in a burns unit, with drug withdrawal.
options: The percentage of body-surface-area epidermal detachment
questions.q: The difference between SJS and TEN is based mainly on:

questions.explain: CREST = Calcinosis, Raynaud, oEsophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasia (limited systemic sclerosis, anti-centromere antibody). Raynaud is often the first feature.
questions.q: Which finding favours systemic sclerosis / CREST?

questions.explain: DLE = chronic photosensitive plaques with follicular plugging, scaling and scarring (including scarring alopecia) and dyspigmentation. Only a minority progress to systemic lupus.
options: Scarring alopecia with atrophic, pigmented plaques
questions.q: Discoid lupus erythematosus typically causes:

questions.explain: A furuncle is a deep infection of the hair follicle, almost always due to S. aureus (not Streptococcus). Coalescence of several furuncles forms a carbuncle.
questions.q: The commonest organism in a furuncle (boil) is:

questions.explain: Common, plantar and anogenital warts are caused by HPV. Most cutaneous warts are benign and self-limiting; certain mucosal HPV types (16, 18) are oncogenic.
options: They are caused by human papillomavirus
They are premalignant and should always be excised
They are caused by a poxvirus
They fluoresce coral-red under Wood’s light
questions.q: Which statement about warts (verrucae) is correct?

questions.explain: Koebnerisation occurs in psoriasis, lichen planus, vitiligo and warts, among others. BCC is a malignancy and does not koebnerise.
questions.q: Koebner phenomenon (lesions appearing at sites of trauma) is typically seen in all EXCEPT:

questions.explain: Permethrin 5% applied to the whole body is first line; treat close contacts simultaneously and launder bedding/clothing. Oral ivermectin is used for crusted or resistant cases.
questions.q: First-line topical treatment for uncomplicated scabies is:

questions.explain: Vitiligo = autoimmune loss of melanocytes → sharply demarcated depigmented (chalk-white) macules that koebnerise; associated with thyroid and other autoimmune disease.
options: Autoimmune destruction of melanocytes causing well-demarcated depigmented macules
A fungal infection causing scaly hypopigmented patches
questions.q: Which best describes vitiligo?

questions.explain: Suspected melanoma should have a full-thickness excisional biopsy so Breslow thickness can be measured accurately; shave biopsy risks transecting the tumour and losing depth information.
options: Excisional biopsy allowing measurement of Breslow thickness
Shave biopsy of the surface only
Punch biopsy of adjacent normal skin
questions.q: The most appropriate biopsy/measurement to determine melanoma prognosis is:

questions.explain: Modern psoriasis biologics target TNF-α, IL-17 (secukinumab/ixekizumab), IL-23 (guselkumab) or IL-12/23 (ustekinumab). They are highly effective for extensive disease and psoriatic arthritis.
questions.q: Which drug class is a mainstay of moderate-to-severe plaque psoriasis by targeting IL-17 or IL-23?

questions.explain: Impetigo: superficial staphylococcal/streptococcal infection with characteristic honey-coloured crusts, common around the nose/mouth in children; treat with topical or oral antibiotics.
questions.q: A honey-coloured crusted plaque around a child’s nose and mouth is most consistent with:

questions.explain: Itch (pruritus) is an essential criterion for atopic dermatitis, alongside a typical age-related distribution, chronic relapsing course and personal/family atopy.
questions.q: Which is a major (essential) diagnostic criterion of atopic dermatitis?

questions.explain: Cellulitis is a deeper dermal/subcutaneous infection with an ill-defined spreading border, whereas erysipelas is more superficial with a sharply raised, demarcated edge. Both are usually streptococcal/staphylococcal and can cause systemic upset.
options: Cellulitis involves the deeper dermis/subcutis with a less well-defined border
Cellulitis has a sharply raised demarcated edge
Cellulitis is caused by a virus
questions.q: Which best distinguishes cellulitis from erysipelas?

papers.subtitle: Balanced mock · 30 MCQs
papers.title: Prediction Paper 2

papers.note: Focuses on high-yield topics that have NOT featured heavily in the papers/recalls you have but are fair game — genodermatoses, nail signs, drug eruptions, phototherapy, skin signs of systemic disease. Predicted content, not official questions.

questions.explain: Seborrhoeic dermatitis (greasy scale of scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, chest) is associated with commensal Malassezia; it is more severe/extensive in Parkinson’s disease and HIV. Treat with antifungal shampoos ± mild steroid.
questions.q: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is linked to overgrowth of which organism?

questions.explain: HS = chronic follicular occlusion in apocrine-bearing intertriginous sites → painful nodules, sinus tracts and scarring (staged by Hurley). Associated with smoking, obesity and metabolic syndrome.
questions.q: Hidradenitis suppurativa characteristically affects:

questions.explain: The Fitzpatrick phototypes (I–VI) grade constitutive pigmentation and tanning/burning tendency; lower types (I–II) burn easily and carry higher skin-cancer risk.
questions.q: The Fitzpatrick classification is used to grade:

questions.explain: Tuberous sclerosis: hypopigmented ash-leaf macules, facial angiofibromas, shagreen patch and periungual fibromas, with CNS/renal involvement. (Café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling belong to NF1.)
questions.q: The ash-leaf macule, adenoma sebaceum (facial angiofibromas) and periungual (Koenen) fibromas are features of:

questions.explain: NF1: ≥6 café-au-lait macules, axillary/inguinal freckling (Crowe’s sign), cutaneous neurofibromas and Lisch nodules of the iris. Autosomal dominant, chromosome 17 (neurofibromin).
questions.q: Axillary freckling (Crowe’s sign) and multiple café-au-lait macules point to:

questions.explain: XP = inherited failure of nucleotide-excision repair of UV-induced DNA damage → extreme photosensitivity and numerous cutaneous malignancies from childhood. Strict photoprotection is essential.
questions.q: Xeroderma pigmentosum predisposes to multiple early skin cancers because of a defect in:

questions.explain: Koilonychia (concave “spoon” nails) is a classic sign of chronic iron deficiency. Pitting/oil-drop = psoriasis; splinter haemorrhages = trauma or infective endocarditis.
questions.q: Which nail sign is associated with iron-deficiency anaemia?

questions.explain: Beau’s lines are transverse grooves from temporary arrest of nail matrix growth during systemic illness, high fever or chemotherapy; their position dates the insult.
questions.q: Transverse ridges across the nail plate reflecting a temporary halt in growth (e.g. after severe illness) are called:

questions.explain: A fixed drug eruption recurs at the same site(s) on re-exposure to the culprit drug (often sulfonamides, NSAIDs, tetracyclines), leaving post-inflammatory pigmentation.
options: A round dusky plaque recurring at the same site with each drug exposure
questions.q: A fixed drug eruption is best characterised by:

questions.explain: DRESS = a severe delayed reaction (anticonvulsants, allopurinol, sulfonamides) with widespread rash, fever, facial oedema, eosinophilia and organ involvement (liver, kidney). Onset is often 2–6 weeks after starting the drug.
options: Rash, fever, eosinophilia and internal-organ (e.g. hepatic) involvement
questions.q: DRESS syndrome (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) typically features:

questions.explain: Scalp ringworm needs systemic (oral) antifungals (e.g. terbinafine, griseofulvin) because topical agents cannot penetrate the follicle; confirm with KOH/culture. Some Microsporum species fluoresce green on Wood’s light.
options: KOH microscopy/culture and ORAL antifungal therapy
questions.q: Tinea capitis is best diagnosed/managed by:

questions.explain: In pityriasis versicolor, KOH of scale shows short curved hyphae plus round spores — the classic “spaghetti-and-meatballs.” (Multinucleate giant cells on a Tzanck smear indicate herpes.)
options: Short hyphae with clusters of spores in pityriasis versicolor
questions.q: Which describes the KOH “spaghetti-and-meatballs” appearance?

questions.explain: The Tzanck smear from a blister base shows multinucleated giant cells in herpetic infections (HSV, VZV). It is a rapid bedside test, now often replaced by PCR.
questions.q: A Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant cells supports a diagnosis of:

questions.explain: Condylomata lata are highly infectious moist papules of secondary syphilis; do not confuse them with condylomata acuminata (HPV genital warts). Secondary syphilis also causes a rash involving the palms and soles.
questions.q: Condylomata lata (broad, moist, wart-like papules) are a manifestation of:

questions.explain: Secondary syphilis produces a widespread copper-coloured maculopapular rash that characteristically involves the palms and soles, often with mucous patches, lymphadenopathy and condylomata lata.
options: It classically involves the palms and soles
questions.q: Which is TRUE of the rash of secondary syphilis?

questions.explain: Despite the name, lupus pernio is a specific sign of sarcoidosis (not lupus), associated with chronic disease and upper-respiratory involvement.
questions.q: Lupus pernio, a violaceous indurated plaque of the nose/cheeks, is a cutaneous marker of:

questions.explain: Kaposi sarcoma (HHV-8) presents as violaceous patches/plaques/nodules; the aggressive epidemic form is AIDS-defining. Cherry (Campbell de Morgan) angiomas are trivial benign papules.
questions.q: Which vascular lesion is caused by human herpesvirus-8 and is an AIDS-defining tumour?

questions.explain: EM = symmetrical target lesions on the extremities, most often triggered by HSV (also Mycoplasma, drugs). It is distinct from SJS/TEN, which are predominantly drug-induced with prominent mucosal necrosis.
questions.q: Erythema multiforme is most classically triggered by:

questions.explain: Limited plaque psoriasis is managed first with topical corticosteroid plus a vitamin-D analogue (e.g. calcipotriol); phototherapy, then systemic/biologic agents are escalated for extensive or resistant disease.
questions.q: The most appropriate long-term management of chronic plaque psoriasis with 5% body-surface involvement is initially:

questions.explain: Mohs offers complete margin assessment with maximal tissue conservation — ideal for high-risk or recurrent BCC/SCC in cosmetically/functionally sensitive sites such as the face.
options: High-risk facial non-melanoma skin cancer where tissue sparing and margin control matter
questions.q: Mohs micrographic surgery is particularly indicated for:

questions.explain: Tetracyclines (especially doxycycline), thiazides, amiodarone, fluoroquinolones and some NSAIDs cause photosensitivity. Patients should be counselled on photoprotection.
questions.q: A photosensitising drug commonly implicated in drug-induced photosensitivity is:

questions.explain: Broad-spectrum sunscreen (UVA+UVB) with photoprotective behaviour (shade, clothing, avoiding peak sun) is the cornerstone of preventing actinic damage, photoageing and skin cancer.
questions.q: The mainstay of preventing UV-induced skin cancer and photoageing is:

questions.explain: Phototherapy (NB-UVB, or psoralen + UVA = PUVA) treats widespread inflammatory disease such as psoriasis, atopic eczema and vitiligo. Long-term PUVA increases skin-cancer risk.
questions.q: Narrowband UVB and PUVA phototherapy are used mainly for:

questions.explain: A halo (Sutton) naevus has a symmetrical depigmented rim from an immune response; the central naevus often involutes. It is benign, though any atypia warrants review.
options: A symmetric surrounding rim of depigmentation with a central naevus that regresses
questions.q: Which feature suggests a melanocytic naevus is a benign “halo” (Sutton) naevus rather than melanoma?

questions.explain: Methotrexate can cause hepatotoxicity, myelosuppression and pneumonitis; monitor FBC, LFTs and renal function, co-prescribe folic acid, and avoid in pregnancy (teratogenic).
questions.q: Methotrexate, used in severe psoriasis, requires monitoring principally for:

questions.explain: In infancy eczema favours the face/extensors, but from childhood onward it becomes flexural (elbow and knee creases, wrists, neck), with lichenification from chronic scratching.
questions.q: The characteristic distribution of atopic dermatitis in older children and adults is:

questions.explain: Angioedema is deep dermal/subcutaneous swelling (lips, eyelids, tongue, larynx) that can threaten the airway; it may accompany urticaria or occur alone (e.g. ACE-inhibitor or hereditary C1-inhibitor deficiency).
options: Involves deeper dermal/subcutaneous swelling, often of lips/eyelids/airway
questions.q: Angioedema differs from urticaria mainly in that it:

questions.explain: Lichen planopilaris (and discoid lupus, folliculitis decalvans) destroy follicles → scarring alopecia with loss of follicular ostia. Alopecia areata, telogen effluvium and androgenetic alopecia are non-scarring.
questions.q: Which of the following is a scarring (cicatricial) alopecia?

questions.explain: Necrolytic migratory erythema is a paraneoplastic sign of a pancreatic glucagonoma (with weight loss, diabetes and anaemia) — an example of a skin sign of internal disease.
questions.q: Necrolytic migratory erythema, an eruption of migrating erosive plaques around the mouth and groin, is a clue to:

questions.explain: Acanthosis nigricans usually reflects insulin resistance/obesity; sudden extensive onset in a thin adult can be paraneoplastic (classically gastric adenocarcinoma). It is another skin marker of systemic disease.
options: Insulin resistance / obesity (and, if abrupt, internal malignancy)
questions.q: Acanthosis nigricans — velvety hyperpigmented thickening of the axillae and neck — is most often associated with:

papers.subtitle: Under-tested but examinable · 30 MCQs
papers.title: Prediction Paper 3

subject: dermatology
